title: Cardiac relaxation: Na+/Ca2+ exchanger
vignette: A 44-year-old man with progressive dyspnea is diagnosed with dilated cardiomyopathy. Despite optimal medical therapy, he continues to have symptoms and disease progression is noted. He undergoes cardiac transplantation after a suitable donor becomes available. Permission is obtained from the patient to study his diseased heart for intracellular calcium regulation. Microelectrodes placed into cardiac muscle cells detect a rapid decrease in cytoplasmic calcium level immediately preceding relaxation. Which of the following proteins is most likely responsible for the observed change in electrolyte levels?

clues.text: progressive dyspnea is diagnosed with dilated cardiomyopathy
clues.note: Dilated cardiomyopathy is just the reason this heart is available to study. It is background context — the testable point is normal cardiac calcium physiology, not the disease.
This flags the topic: how cardiac myocytes handle Ca2+ across the contraction-relaxation cycle. Frame the answer around calcium entry vs. removal.

clues.text: rapid decrease in cytoplasmic calcium level immediately preceding relaxation
clues.note: The key event: relaxation requires cytosolic Ca2+ to FALL rapidly, i.e., calcium must be REMOVED from the cytoplasm. The answer must be a calcium-efflux/reuptake mechanism, not a calcium-entry/release one.

whatMatters: Almost the entire vignette (transplant, cardiomyopathy) is window dressing. The single testable point: what protein makes cytoplasmic Ca2+ drop right before relaxation? That is a calcium-REMOVAL step. The two removers are the Na+/Ca2+ exchanger (NCX) and SERCA — and of the listed options, only the Na+/Ca2+ exchanger removes calcium. Answer B.
strategy: Sort every calcium protein into one of three buckets: RAISES cytosolic Ca2+ (L-type channel influx, ryanodine-receptor release), BINDS Ca2+ (troponin C, calmodulin), or REMOVES Ca2+ (NCX efflux, SERCA reuptake). 'Relaxation / falling calcium' = the removal bucket. Don't grab a famous calcium protein without checking which direction it moves calcium.

eliminations.label: calmodulin — senses calcium, doesn't pump it
eliminations.detail: WHAT IT IS: a ubiquitous Ca2+-binding messenger protein; in SMOOTH muscle it activates myosin light-chain kinase and it regulates many Ca2+-dependent enzymes. ❌ WHY IT FAILS: it binds/relays calcium but does not extrude it from cardiac cytoplasm; relaxation needs an efflux pump. ✅ Correct if: the question asked about smooth-muscle contraction or a calcium sensor.

eliminations.label: ryanodine receptors — RELEASE calcium (your pick)
eliminations.detail: WHAT IT IS: the sarcoplasmic-reticulum calcium-RELEASE channel (RyR2) that drives calcium-induced calcium release, RAISING cytoplasmic Ca2+ to start contraction. ❌ WHY IT FAILS: that is the opposite direction — RyR raises cytosolic calcium; relaxation requires a rapid DECREASE. The trap is reaching for the famous SR calcium channel. ✅ Correct if: asked what increases cytoplasmic Ca2+ to initiate contraction.

eliminations.label: troponin C — binds calcium, doesn't remove it
eliminations.detail: WHAT IT IS: the thin-filament protein that BINDS cytosolic Ca2+ to move tropomyosin and permit actin-myosin cross-bridging. ❌ WHY IT FAILS: it is a contraction switch, not an efflux mechanism; binding calcium doesn't lower cytoplasmic calcium. ✅ Correct if: asked which protein calcium binds to start cross-bridge cycling.

eliminations.label: voltage-dependent calcium channels — let calcium IN
eliminations.detail: WHAT IT IS: L-type channels open during the plateau (phase 2) to let Ca2+ INTO the myocyte, triggering calcium-induced calcium release. ❌ WHY IT FAILS: they RAISE cytoplasmic calcium at the start of contraction, not lower it before relaxation. ✅ Correct if: asked about the initial calcium influx of the cardiac action potential.

reasoning: Relaxation requires cytoplasmic Ca2+ to fall rapidly = calcium must be removed from the cytosol
Two systems remove cytosolic Ca2+: the Na+/Ca2+ exchanger (extrudes Ca2+) and SERCA (pumps Ca2+ back into the SR)
Of the listed options, only the Na+/Ca2+ exchanger lowers cytoplasmic calcium
mechanism: Cardiac relaxation begins when cytoplasmic Ca2+ is rapidly cleared. The two main routes are the sarcolemmal Na+/Ca2+ exchanger (NCX), which uses the inward Na+ gradient to extrude one Ca2+ in exchange for three Na+, and SERCA, which actively pumps Ca2+ back into the sarcoplasmic reticulum. As cytosolic Ca2+ falls, it dissociates from troponin C, tropomyosin re-blocks actin, and the myofilaments relax. Of the listed proteins, only NCX removes Ca2+ from the cytoplasm; the others raise cytosolic Ca2+ (L-type channels, ryanodine receptors) or merely bind it (troponin C, calmodulin).

difficulty.label: 📚 Pure knowledge gap
difficulty.message: First Aid Physio > cardiac/muscle excitation-contraction coupling: L-type Ca2+ influx -> RyR (calcium-induced calcium release) -> troponin C -> contraction; relaxation via NCX + SERCA (and sarcolemmal Ca2+-ATPase). Know which step each protein performs.

difficulty.label: 🧭 Understanding the question
difficulty.message: The ask is 'what lowers cytoplasmic Ca2+ right before relaxation' = a calcium-removal step. Bucket each option as raises / binds / removes calcium, then pick the remover.

difficulty.label: 🔤 Vocabulary
difficulty.message: Unsure on NCX, SERCA, ryanodine receptor (RyR2), calcium-induced calcium release, troponin C, calmodulin? Tell me which and I'll explain it.

difficulty.label: 🧲 Pulled by a distractor
difficulty.message: Ryanodine receptor (C) is the trap — it's the famous SR calcium channel, but it RELEASES Ca2+ (raises cytoplasm) for contraction; relaxation needs efflux (NCX).

difficulty.label: ✍️ Something else
difficulty.message: Bank the split: influx/release = L-type + RyR (contraction); efflux/reuptake = NCX + SERCA (relaxation).

keywords.front: Rapid fall in cytoplasmic Ca2+ before cardiac relaxation

keywords.back: 3 Na+ in : 1 Ca2+ out (uses the Na+ gradient)
Pumps Ca2+ back into the SR (ATP-dependent)
Binds cytosolic Ca2+ to start cross-bridge cycling

firstAid.detail: Physio > cardiac muscle relaxation: NCX (3 Na+ / 1 Ca2+) + SERCA remove cytosolic Ca2+; falling Ca2+ releases troponin C.
firstAid.location: Physio > cardiac muscle relaxation: NCX (3 Na+ / 1 Ca2+) + SERCA remove cytosolic Ca2+; falling Ca2+ releases troponin C.

firstAid.detail: Physio > L-type channel (influx), RyR2 (CICR release), troponin C (binds Ca2+), calmodulin (smooth muscle / MLCK).
firstAid.location: Physio > L-type channel (influx), RyR2 (CICR release), troponin C (binds Ca2+), calmodulin (smooth muscle / MLCK).

firstAid.detail: CV path > systolic dysfunction, eccentric hypertrophy; causes: idiopathic, alcohol, myocarditis, peripartum, doxorubicin, hemochromatosis.
firstAid.location: CV path > systolic dysfunction, eccentric hypertrophy; causes: idiopathic, alcohol, myocarditis, peripartum, doxorubicin, hemochromatosis.

memoryHook: To RELAX, the cell must get calcium OUT: NCX pumps it out, SERCA stuffs it back in the SR. RyR/L-type bring calcium IN (contraction).
